text: The video shows various types of endometriosis lesions, including black, white, and red lesions, located posterior to the uterus. The uterus is visibly moving, with the left ovary and fallopian tubes on both sides clearly observable.
The surgeon lifts the tissue, revealing a yellowish area indicating the beginning of the bowel, and shows the back of the cervix where it meets the rectum.
The surgeon elevates a nodule that is being progressively dissected from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon elevates tissue from the left pelvic sidewall, revealing the yellowish appearance of the bowel at the beginning of the dissection.
The surgeon points out a hemorrhagic collection at the margin, associated with endometriosis, while distinguishing black areas as old hemorrhage rather than active endometriosis.
The surgical device cuts through adipose tissue, visibly eliminating lesions in the area.
The surgeon prepares to remove a large nodule, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes a large nodule of endometriosis, excising it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon removes a nodule on the left side, followed by the removal of another nodule on the right side, completing this stage of the surgery.
